title: Custom & Combined Loss Functions
concept: For advanced multi-task networks that predict multiple targets at once, we combine different loss metrics into a single weighted total loss score to guide the optimizer.

exercises.prompt: Recap: Why is KL Divergence called a divergence rather than a distance?

options.text: It is asymmetric, meaning the score of P from Q is not equal to Q from P.
It can only be calculated on flat 2D grids.
It does not use gradients during training.

exercises.explain: Correct! KL Divergence is asymmetric, which separates it from standard distance metrics.

cards.title: The multi-talented athlete
cards.body: If you are training a triathlete, you can't just measure their running speed. You must measure swimming, cycling, and running. If they are fast runners but slow swimmers, you need a single total score that balances all three events. In advanced deep learning, we combine different loss scores into a single weighted total loss.

cards.title: Combined Loss: Multi-task training
cards.body: A combined loss function blends different loss metrics (e.g. MSE and Categorical Cross-Entropy) into a single score. This is used in **multi-task learning**, where one neural network makes multiple different predictions from the same input.

cards.title: Grading a project
cards.body: Think of a combined loss like a final grade on a class project. The teacher scores: 70% on content accuracy, 20% on visual design, and 10% on spelling. You add them up to get one final grade. During training, the network's weights adjust to improve this combined total grade, rather than just one part.

cards.title: Self-driving car targets
cards.body: A self-driving car model must predict two things: 1) **Steering angle** (regression target, uses MSE loss), and 2) **Stop sign detected** (binary classification, uses Cross-Entropy). We sum them: `Total Loss = (Steering MSE) + (Stop Sign BCE)`. The optimizer minimizes this combined score.

cards.title: Weighting the parts is essential
cards.body: You might think you can just add different losses together directly. In reality, different losses scale differently — an MSE of $10,000$ will completely drown out a Cross-Entropy loss of $0.5$. We must multiply each part by a weight factor (e.g. `0.001 * MSE`) to balance their influence during training.

cards.title: Combined Loss Architecture
cards.body: How multiple losses merge into one training signal:

left.text:
Continuous angle prediction.
Uses MSE Loss.
Multiplied by Weight A (e.g. 0.01).

right.text:
Binary classification.
Uses Cross-Entropy Loss.
Multiplied by Weight B (e.g. 1.0).

exercises.prompt: When does a neural network require a combined loss function?

options.text: In multi-task learning, when one network makes multiple different predictions (like regression and classification) from the same input.
When the dataset is completely clean with zero missing values.
When we want to disable all activation functions.
Only when training on CPUs instead of GPUs.

exercises.explain: Correct! Combined loss is used to balance multiple different prediction targets in a single network.

exercises.prompt: True or false: When combining different loss functions (like MSE and Cross-Entropy), we must use weight factors to prevent one loss from dominating the other.
exercises.explain: True! Losses scale differently, so weights are required to balance their influence on training gradients.

exercises.sentence: A single network that predicts both house price (regression) and sale probability (classification) is an example of ___ learning.
exercises.explain: Multi-task learning involves training a single model to perform multiple distinct prediction tasks.

exercises.prompt: Which formula represents a combined loss function for a model predicting object bounding box (MSE) and category (CCE)?

options.text: Total Loss = (0.3 * Bounding Box MSE) + (0.7 * Category CCE)
Total Loss = Bounding Box MSE / Category CCE
Total Loss = Bounding Box MSE only

exercises.explain: Correct! Summing the weighted components of both targets represents a combined loss.

exercises.prompt: You build a model to detect cancer cells in medical scans. The model outputs: 1. Cell coordinate box (uses MSE), and 2. Is malignant? (uses BCE). During training, the MSE loss is high, causing the model to ignore malignant classification errors. How do you resolve this?

options.text: Adjust the combined loss weights, increasing the weight factor for the malignant BCE loss to balance its influence against the larger MSE scale.
Delete the BCE loss and use MSE for both tasks.
Increase the learning rate to make the MSE loss smaller.
Switch the activation function to ReLU at the output layer.

exercises.explain: Exactly! If one loss dominates, we adjust the weight factors in the combined loss function to scale their gradient signals, ensuring the optimizer pays attention to both tasks. Next, let's look at the decision matrix for choosing the right loss function!
